text: Today we're going to discuss a case of a robotic repair of an incarcerated right-sided diaphragmatic hernia after liver resection. There are no conflicts of interest and we have nothing to disclose. Right-sided diaphragmatic hernias are most often due to blunt or penetrating trauma. However, they are a complication of liver resection and liver transplant in approximately two to three percent of patients. They have a high risk of incarceration and strangulation, so all right-sided diaphragmatic hernias should be repaired regardless of symptoms.

Our case was a forty-three-year-old female who was three years status post large hepatic adenoma resection. She presented to the emergency room with several months of abdominal discomfort and diarrhea, with acutely worsening pain over the last several days. She now had eight out of ten constant diffuse abdominal pain and nausea. As mentioned, past medical history seems for hepatic adenoma resection hypothyroidism, anxiety, and depression. She's otherwise a non-smoker and a social alcohol user. On physical exam, her vitals were stable in the emergency department. On abdominal exam, she was soft, non-distended, tender to palpation in the right upper quadrant, and she had her prior chevron incision scar. Her labs were otherwise unremarkable.

A CAT scan of the abdomen and pelvis was obtained with IV contrasts. which clearly showed a right-sided diaphragmatic hernia near the old hepatic adenoma resection site. The hernia itself contained transverse colon and was distended proximally and decompressed distally, indicating obstruction. Here's a coronal view of the incarcerated hernia. And lastly, here's a sagittal view of the incarcerated hernia.

The surgeon places a 12mm Hassan port in the right upper quadrant and three 8mm robotic ports, two along the old chevron incision on the left side and one in the epigastric area. Adhesions from prior surgery are lysed to gain exposure. The hernia in the diaphragm is visualized, showing the transverse colon and omentum protruding through the defect. The anterior portion of the hernia sac is dissected using scissors attached to monopolar electrocautery. The diaphragm is opened with a harmonic scalpel to assist in reducing the hernia, causing loss of pneumoperitoneum as air escapes into the pleural space. A diamond flex liver retractor is placed through the assistant port to elevate the diaphragm and improve visualization of the hernia defect.

The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, including the transverse colon and omentum, back into the abdominal cavity through the enlarged defect in the diaphragm. The defect measures approximately five centimeters in length.

The surgeon visualizes the lung through the primary diaphragmatic defect. A secondary defect lateral to the primary one is observed, containing incarcerated omentum, which is reduced. Using a PEDS V-lock suture, the surgeon begins the primary repair of the diaphragmatic defect, starting anteriorly to elevate the defect for better visualization. Imbricated suture bites are taken to strengthen the closure.

The surgeon moves the liver retractor inferiorly to visualize the lower aspect of the diaphragmatic defect. An 8 cm by 5 cm Allomax biological mesh is placed over the closed hernia defect to reinforce the repair, anchored circumferentially using a V-lock suture.

Postoperatively, the diet was advanced on postoperative day one and patient was discharged home on postoperative day two. In conclusion, right side diaphragmatic hernia is commonly seen after trauma, liver resection, and liver transplants. These hernias should be repaired and surgically fit patients due to high risk of incarceration and strangulation. Peer repair can be approached robotically through the abdomen, with or without mesh, depending on the clinical situation, such as hernia size, multiplicity of defects, and age of patient.
